pattern: Sorting the skills and pairing the smallest with the largest is the only pairing that can make every team's total equal, so one pass checking that every such pair sums to the same target -- while accumulating the product sums -- both validates feasibility and produces the answer together.
hints: If every team must share the exact same total skill, that shared total has to equal the overall sum divided by the number of teams -- compute that target first.
Sort the skills. The only way to reliably hit a fixed pair-sum repeatedly is to pair the smallest remaining value with the largest remaining value each time.

Walk inward from both ends of the sorted array simultaneously: if any pair's sum doesn't match the target total, no valid division exists and you should return -1; otherwise add that pair's product to a running answer.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, no valid division exists
minimum possible input, two equal-skill players
already sorted ascending, still must pair ends not adjacent players
all players share identical skill, every pairing works
one player's skill is far outside what any pairing could balance
larger even roster with multiple repeated skill values

stress.description: half the trials build a guaranteed-balanceable roster from random pairs summing to a common target; the other half are fully random and often infeasible

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def dividePlayers(self, skill):
        skill.sort()
        n = len(skill)
        target = skill[0] + skill[-1]
        chemistry = 0
        left, right = 0, n - 1
        while left < right:
            if skill[left] + skill[right] != target:
                return -1
            chemistry += skill[left] * skill[right]
            left += 1
            right -= 1
        return chemistry
